data.name: Tokamak (Magnetic Confinement)
data.approach: Toroidal magnetic confinement - donut-shaped chamber with helical magnetic fields confining plasma
data.organization: ITER, CFS/SPARC, EAST, KSTAR, JET, DIII-D
data.status: Most mature fusion approach; ITER under construction; CFS targeting Q≥2 by 2027
data.milestone: JET 69 MJ (2023); ITER targeting Q≥10 by 2035
data.description: The most studied and funded fusion approach. A tokamak uses strong toroidal and poloidal magnetic fields to confine a donut-shaped plasma at 100-200 million°C. ITER is the flagship project. CFS's compact tokamak using HTS magnets could accelerate the timeline. The main challenges are plasma disruptions, divertor heat loads, and tritium breeding.

data.name: Stellarator
data.approach: Twisted toroidal magnetic confinement - inherently stable 3D magnetic field geometry
data.organization: Max Planck IPP (Wendelstein 7-X), University of Wisconsin (HSX)
data.status: W7-X demonstrating improved confinement; proof-of-principle for stellarator optimization
data.milestone: W7-X achieved 1.3 GJ energy turnover and 8-minute plasmas (2023)
data.timeline: Commercial stellarator: 2050s (longer than tokamak but potentially more reliable)
data.description: Stellarators use complex 3D magnetic field shapes that are inherently stable (no plasma current needed), eliminating disruption risk. Wendelstein 7-X in Germany is the world's largest stellarator and has demonstrated that optimized designs can achieve tokamak-like confinement. Stellarators are harder to build but potentially more reliable for steady-state power plants.

data.name: Inertial Confinement Fusion (ICF)
data.approach: Compress and heat tiny fuel pellets with lasers or X-rays to achieve fusion conditions
data.organization: LLNL/NIF, Omega (LLE), LMJ (France), First Light Fusion
data.status: Ignition achieved at NIF (2022); not yet a viable power plant approach
data.timeline: IFE power plant: 2040s-2050s; significant engineering challenges remain
data.description: ICF uses powerful lasers to compress a DT fuel capsule to extreme density and temperature. NIF achieved ignition in 2022, proving the physics works. However, converting this to a power plant requires: 10 Hz repetition rate (NIF fires once/day), efficient laser drivers, target manufacturing at scale, and chamber design. IFE research is expanding with new driver approaches.

data.name: Field-Reversed Configuration (FRC)
data.approach: Compact self-organized plasma structure with closed magnetic field lines; no central penetration needed
data.status: TAE Norman at 30M°C; Helion building Polaris; Microsoft PPA signed for 2028
data.milestone: Helion: first commercial fusion PPA; TAE: sustained FRC plasmas
data.description: FRC creates a self-contained plasma ring with its own magnetic field, requiring minimal external magnets. This enables compact, potentially much cheaper fusion devices. Helion uses pulsed FRC with direct energy conversion (no steam). TAE targets aneutronic p-B11 fusion. Both are high-risk, high-reward approaches with aggressive timelines.

data.name: Z-Pinch / Magneto-Inertial
data.approach: Use electrical current through plasma to create self-confining magnetic field; or compress magnetized plasma with imploding liner
data.status: Zap Energy at 37M°C; Sandia MagLIF demonstrating significant neutron yields
data.milestone: Zap: sheared-flow Z-pinch at fusion-relevant conditions; Sandia: MagLIF scaling favorably
data.description: Z-pinch is the simplest fusion concept: run a large current through plasma, and the resulting magnetic field confines it. The challenge is instability. Zap Energy solves this with sheared flow (differential plasma velocity). Sandia's MagLIF uses a laser to preheat magnetized fuel before Z-pinch compression. Both approaches avoid expensive magnetic coils.

data.name: Aneutronic Fusion (p-B11)
data.approach: Proton-boron fusion produces charged particles (no neutrons), enabling direct energy conversion
data.organization: TAE Technologies, HB11 Energy, LPP Fusion
data.status: Research phase; requires 10x higher temperature than D-T fusion (~1 billion°C)
data.milestone: TAE: 30M°C achieved; need ~1 billion°C for p-B11
data.description: Aneutronic fusion using hydrogen and boron-11 produces only charged alpha particles, eliminating neutron damage and activation. This enables direct energy conversion (electricity from charged particles, no steam cycle) with potential >80% efficiency. The catch: it requires temperatures of ~1 billion°C, 10x higher than D-T fusion. TAE and HB11 are pursuing different approaches to reach these conditions.
